cards.definition: a temporary store of limited capacity — holds around 7 items for up to 30 seconds
a permanent store of unlimited capacity and duration — holds memories for a lifetime
converting information into a form that can be stored in memory — e.g. acoustic (sound), visual (image), semantic (meaning)
the amount of information a memory store can hold — STM: ~7 items; LTM: unlimited
how long a memory store retains information — STM: up to 30 seconds; LTM: lifetime
a model proposing memory flows through three stores: sensory register, STM, and LTM
repeating information to keep it in STM or transfer it to LTM — maintenance or elaborative rehearsal
accessing and bringing stored information back into conscious awareness
the inability to recall stored information — causes include interference, decay, and lack of retrieval cues
forgetting caused by other memories disrupting recall — proactive (old memories) or retroactive (new memories)
the process of interpreting sensory information to make sense of the world
raw sensory data received by the sense organs before it is interpreted
visual signals that help us perceive distance and depth — e.g. size, overlap, linear perspective
cues that can be seen with one eye — e.g. relative size, texture gradient, linear perspective
cues that require both eyes — e.g. binocular disparity (slightly different images from each eye)
the tendency to perceive things in a particular way based on past experience, emotion, or motivation
when perception does not match physical reality — e.g. the Müller-Lyer illusion
debate over whether perception is innate (nature) or shaped by experience and culture (nurture)
the theory that perception is based directly on information in the environment — no prior knowledge needed (nature)
the theory that perception is an active process using prior knowledge and experience to interpret sensory data (nurture)
four stages: sensorimotor (0–2), preoperational (2–7), concrete operational (7–11), formal operational (11+)
a mental framework or category used to organise and interpret information — updated through assimilation and accommodation
fitting new information into an existing schema
changing an existing schema to fit new information that doesn't match it
the understanding that objects continue to exist even when they cannot be seen — develops in the sensorimotor stage
the inability to see things from another person's point of view — characteristic of the preoperational stage
the understanding that quantity stays the same even if its appearance changes — develops in the concrete operational stage
the idea that students learn best when new information is connected to things they already know
a strong emotional bond between a child and caregiver — important for emotional development
the theory that children have an innate need to form one primary attachment — the caregiver provides a secure base
a research method where one variable is changed to see its effect on another, while other variables are controlled
the variable that is deliberately changed by the researcher in an experiment
the variable that is measured to see if it has been affected by the IV
a variable that is kept the same to ensure a fair test
a clear, testable prediction about what will happen in a study
an experiment conducted in a controlled environment — high control but low ecological validity
an experiment conducted in a real-world setting — higher ecological validity but less control
an in-depth investigation of a single individual, group, or event — produces rich data but hard to generalise
rules from the British Psychological Society governing research — including informed consent, right to withdraw, and debriefing
the extent to which research findings can be applied to real-world situations
